html:
Hermann Conring, born on November 9, 1606, and died on December 12, 1681, was a German scientist.

Conring studied theology and medicine in Leiden. In 1632, he became a professor of natural philosophy at the University of Helmstedt, but in 1637, he moved to the medical faculty there. King Louis XVI greatly admired Conring, and Queen Christina appointed him her personal physician from 1650 to 1651. Conring also received high international recognition for his legal expertise.

Among Conring's medical works, "De sanguinis generatione et motu naturali" (1643) is particularly noteworthy, as it helped spread William Harvey's understanding of the circulatory system. In the field of law, his valuable overview "De origine juris germanici" (1643) stands out.

== Sources == Svensk uppslagsbok. Malmö 1931. Jori, Alberto, Hermann Conring (1606-1681): Der Begründer der deutschen Rechtsgeschichte, with a welcoming address by Kristian Kühl, Tübingen, 2006 ISBN 3-935625-59-6.

== External links == Conring, Hermann in Nordisk familjebok (second edition, 1906)

How certain are we about this?

People from the era of great power were extensively documented in archives and contemporary publications. Most information about who they were and what they did is well-documented; uncertainty lies primarily in interpretation and motivation.
tldr: Hermann Conring was a German scientist who became known for his studies in medicine and law. He worked, among other things, as a physician to Queen Christina.
lattlast:
Hermann Conring was born in 1606 and died in 1681. He was a German scholar who studied both religion and medicine at the University of Leiden. Later, he became a professor at the University of Helmstedt, where he taught natural sciences and medicine.

Conring gained international recognition for his expertise in law, which deals with laws and regulations. He wrote an important book on the history of German law, which was highly regarded by other experts.

In the field of medicine, he helped to disseminate new knowledge about how blood circulates in the body. Due to his extensive knowledge, he was appointed as Queen Christina's personal physician between 1650 and 1651.
